description:
If /etc/at.deny exists, it must have permissions 0600 or more restrictive. To properly set the permissions of /etc/at.deny, run the command: 
$ sudo chmod 0600 /etc/at.deny
rationale:
If the permissions of the at.deny file are not set to 0600 or more restrictive,
the possibility exists for an unauthorized user to view or edit sensitive information.
ocil:
To check the permissions of /etc/at.deny, run the command: 
$ ls -l /etc/at.deny
If properly configured, the output should indicate the following permissions: -rw-------
title: Verify Permissions on /etc/at.deny file
